guest_role: Founder and Managing Partner of Glasswing Ventures (Boston AI-focused fund)
assertiveness_why: Harry builds a tight logical chain of thesis-driven follow-ups (data ownership -> incumbency advantage -> defensibility -> business models) and probes predictions, but never directly disputes or contests any of Seseri's answers.
question_style: Structured and thesis-building — each question extends logically from the guest's prior answer (e.g., moving from data ownership to incumbency advantage to AI business models), plus a prediction-focused segment on ASI and driverless cars. No interruptions or pushback, but clearly plants a chain of reasoning for her to respond to.
tone: Polished and enthusiastic, genuine intellectual curiosity about AI, matey but slightly more formal/topical than personality-driven episodes; heavy sponsor reads (Headspace, Luma).
depth: Fairly technical/strategic for the era — covers data ownership vs. privacy regulation, narrow vs general AI, data incumbency and defensibility, AI-native business models vs SaaS, and disruption-cycle timing (web to social-mobile to AI adoption curves).
harry_quotes: Do you think in terms of ownership these will be in the form of public data sets... or privately owned?
Do you think there is a really significant data incumbency advantage to the likes of... Microsoft or Google or Facebook?
How long do you think until artificial super intelligence comes to the forefront?
learnings: Seseri frames the AI wave as the third major tech disruption cycle after the web and social-mobile, expecting a shorter adoption curve driven by younger, tech-native users.
She argues large incumbents (Microsoft, Google, Facebook) have a data advantage but it's surmountable because incumbents' own bureaucracy and revenue-cannibalization fears slow their response.
She believes proprietary data matters but treating consumers well around data trust can itself become a brand-level barrier to entry.
She sees AI-powered products as reshaping performance and technology within existing monetization models (e.g., still SaaS) rather than requiring wholly new business models.
She predicts winner-take-most (not winner-take-all) dynamics in autonomous vehicles, expecting three or so major players rather than pure consolidation.
notable: Seseri discloses her own portfolio company (Jibo, a social robotics startup) mid-answer as a live example of the category she's betting on.
